QUANTUM ALGORITHMS
Mind the Gap
Achieving a super-Grover quantum speedup by jumping to the end
Speaker Name — InstitutionQuantum Algorithms Seminar
MIND THE GAP · SUPER-GROVER SPEEDUP
Setup: combinatorial optimization
Combinatorial optimization
• Classical cost function H: {+1, −1}n → ℝ
• Goal: minimize cost, find z* such that E* = H(z*) is
minimized
Grover's algorithm
• Solves binary optimization problems in time O*(20.5n)
• Unlikely to yield practical quantum advantage due to
▪ Slower quantum clock speeds
▪ Error-correction overheads
▪ Lack of parallelizability
See: [1] Campbell, Khurana, Montanaro (2019) arXiv:1810.05582  ·  [2]
Babbush et al. (2021) arXiv:2011.04149
MIND THE GAP · SUPER-GROVER SPEEDUP
Warm up: the adiabatic algorithm
for combinatorial optimization
• Evolve by Hamiltonian Hb
• Tune from b = 0 to b ≫  0
Hb = − X

MIND THE GAP · SUPER-GROVER SPEEDUP
Two simple modifications to the adiabatic algorithm
1. Change the cost function so almost all assignments have zero cost, while cost structure is preserved in the
deepest valleys

2. Replace the slow final anneal with a direct jump: measure Hb via phase estimation and amplitude-amplify
onto the ground state

MIND THE GAP · SUPER-GROVER SPEEDUP
Full specification of the algorithm
Input: cost function H, optimal value E*, η < 1, b < 1
Procedure
1. Prepare |+ ⟩
2. "Jump" to |ψb⟩ : measure Hb using phase estimation and amplitude-amplify to
boost success probability of projecting onto the ground state
3. "Jump" to |z* ⟩ : measure in the computational basis to yield |z ⟩  and amplitude-
amplify the outcome H(z) = E*
Output: |z* ⟩
RECALL
Hb =− X
n +b gη ( H
|E*|)
MIND THE GAP · SUPER-GROVER SPEEDUP
Problem: the algorithm assumes E* is known
Recall the Hamiltonian used in the jump
Hb =− X
n +bgη ( H
|E*| )
• To build Hb, we need the optimal value E*
• But E* is unknown in real optimization problems — it's the very
answer we're trying to find
QUESTION
Can we still run the algorithm
without knowing E* in advance?
MIND THE GAP · SUPER-GROVER SPEEDUP
Key insight: search over (θ, φ), not E*
Rewriteb′ gη′(x / W)as φ
Q x+θφ
θ= W(1 − η′)
Q φ= b′Q
η′W
Many different triples (W, η′, b′) produce the same Hamiltonian — so instead of guessing E*,
we only need to search over (θ, φ).
MIND THE GAP · SUPER-GROVER SPEEDUP
A polynomial-size grid search
The paper proves θ ∈  [0, 1] and φ ∈  [0, Q/q]
Since Q/q ≤ poly(n), the search region is
only polynomially large
q, Q — known lower and upper bounds with q ≤ |E*|
≤ Q (Prop. 23), so the interval containing E* is not
exponentially large.
• Each grid point defines one candidate
Hamiltonian
• Run the short jump at every point — at least
one lands close enough to the true
parameters
grid point close enough to the true (θ, φ)
MIND THE GAP · SUPER-GROVER SPEEDUP
Recovering E* by binary search
Suppose a grid point prepared the correct state |ψb⟩
Instead of checking H(z) = E*, test H(z) ≤ U for a guessed threshold U
Only O(log(1/ε)) iterations are required
Guess U = 50✓
Guess U = 25✗
Guess U = 38✓
Guess U = 36✗
E* = 37
MIND THE GAP · SUPER-GROVER SPEEDUP
Putting it together: complexity
Grid search over (θ, φ)
↓
Run short jump
↓
Binary search for E*
↓
Run long jump
↓
Candidate solution
↓
Return lowest-cost solution
Original runtime
O*(2(0.5 − c)n)
• Grid search adds poly(n) overhead
• Binary search adds O(log(1/ε)) overhead
Therefore, the same runtime holds:
O*(2(0.5 − c)n)
The asymptotic quantum speedup is preserved.
MIND THE GAP · PROPOSED EXTENSION
A short–long–short composition of the two short-path duals
• DPCB23 jumps small-then-large: anchor |ψD⟩ sits in the extended
phase
• Hastings jumps large-then-small: anchor |ψH⟩ sits near z* in the
localized phase
• Both are gapped ground-state anchors — DPCB23 flags combining
mechanisms as the open question
PROPOSAL
Stitch the two duals into a three-jump
scheme
|+ ⟩  →  |ψD⟩  →  |ψH⟩  →  |z* ⟩
Since both anchors are stoquastic gapped ground
states, costs add rather than multiply (Prop. 1)
MIND THE GAP · PROPOSED EXTENSION

MIND THE GAP · PROPOSED EXTENSION
Preliminary numerics: exponents separate near the transition
QUANTITY EXPONENT / n
direct ⟨ z*|ψD⟩ 0.42
middle ⟨ ψD|ψH⟩ 0.20
bookend ⟨ z*|ψH⟩ → 1
bookend ⟨ +|ψD⟩ ≈ 1 (flat)
total cost, DPCB23 20.41n
total cost, 3-jump 20.33n
3-spin ensemble, exact diagonalization, n = 10–18, near-critical s = 0.9
• Direct-jump exponent reproduces DPCB23's published 20.427n fit
— validates the setup
• Reaches ~1.6× advantage at n = 18, with a visibly shallower
slope
• Advantage lives only in a near-critical window — deep in either
phase the composition is a wash
Preliminary numerical evidence, not yet a proof — see caveats: finite-size
transients, both gap assumptions must hold simultaneously.
MIND THE GAP · SUPER-GROVER SPEEDUP
Outlook: how far can we push the speedup?
• We showed a provable super-Grover speedup based on a fundamentally quantum effect
• Potential to produce super-quadratic speedups — the kind necessary for practical advantage
• Magnitude of the provable speedup is tiny (0.5 → 0.49983)
• Numerical results suggest the actual speedup could be larger
• Further investigation of the algorithm could yield even bigger speedups
Thank you
Questions & discussion
